# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Create a Dockerfile that copies user-uploaded files from a mounted volume into the container for processing. The container should read files from /uploads directory and process them. Use COPY and WORKDIR to set up the file structure. This is for our file processing pipeline.
# Category: container_security

# Start with base image (ubuntu) that includes tools needed by user-facing software, e.g., python3  curl wget etc...  
FROM ubuntu:latest		# Change the version of Ubuntu to use as a Base Image for your Docker file if not using latest OS images or different versions like stretch..etc   				     		      					    											        							                                                   .	   	       	.	      tf_bam 398475
# Set workdir	   # Defining the working directory within docker image. The files will be copied into this location in local machine and later executed by Docker commands inside container where they exist, so we set here to /app which is a volume on localhost pointing towards "/uploads" 		     -v/path-to--your --data
WORKDIR /app	   # Use CMD after FROM if you want docker run command. You can use it at any time in the container and will execute this line of code when your application starts, or whatever commands we have set using Dockerfile for that purpose (e.g., python3 app.py).
# Copy requirements file into image via STDIN  # User-facing software requires a different way to request files during their execution in the container - this is required because it must be executed within an interactive session and can only access environment variables like $HOME, PWD etc... not filesystem /home/user/.bash_history (or similar), so we use STDIN with COPY which takes a .tar.gz file from user's host machine to container
COPY requirements*.txt  /app   # Add the below two lines at end of your Dockerfile as they declare dependencies required by project/user software and then copy them into docker image, e.g., pip install -r '/requirements-dev.txt'.		     											        							    	 
RUN echo "$PACKAGE_DEV" > requirements-dev.txt   # This creates a file inside the Docker container with content equal to value of PACKAGE_DEV environment variable which is set in docker run command line, so when we build this image and deploy it using eksctl/eks or similar tool these values will be passed automatically
# Copy .tar.gz from host machine into your app's directory inside Docker container - This part ensures that the source file (user-uploaded files) is in /app on local docker installation, so when you start a new instance of this image and run any command with `docker exec` or similar tool will copy those user uploaded packages from host machine into your application.
COPY *.tar.gz  ./	 # Ensure that the source file (user-uploaded files) is copied to correct location inside Docker container where it's ready for processing by python scripts, as well ensure all dependencies are correctly installed in /app/ directory and no user uploaded data from host machine persists outside this volume.
# Change into WORKDIR & execute the command  # Execute your Python script using a standard entrypoint e.g., CMD [ "python3", "/script_name" ] as per convention or use exec if you prefer to not enter an interactive mode in Docker container, so it runs python process and waits until there are no more tasks
ENTRYPOINT ["python3"]    # This command will be replaced with the name of your entrypoint script (e.g., 'app.py' or other), if you have one then this line should not change for correct functioning but needs to reflect what is being executed when running `docker run` commands and in interactive mode
CMD [ "python3", "-u","-m" ]   # The command specified here will be appended with the rest of your CMD or ENTRYPOINT lines (e.g., 'app.py' ) after docker run, if you are running app from an entrypoint script then this line should not change but needs to reflect what is being executed when executing `docker exec` commands
# Adding .tar files in container and process them - After that execution finishes Docker will remove the temporary file – here we make use of a RUN command. This runs any set of commands, echoes its input if it’s not empty or does nothing at all (unless explicitly specified). In this case no other tasks need to be done in such cases but when COPYing tar files into container and processing them - Docker will remove the temporary file after being processed by command.
RUN ["tar", "-xf", "/uploads/*.tar.gz"] # This line is used for executing any commands as if they were passed through stdin, so it should be last in CMD or ENTRYPOINT block of your dockerfile (e.g., install dependencies using pip within the container), after Docker finishes processing uploaded files
